channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Higher Gas Prices Are Hitting Lower-Income Americans the Hardest
item.description: People with lower incomes are driving less and devoting more of their budgets to transport as gas prices soar.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Federal Reserve Bank of New York
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gas prices have soared since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Why Is Gas So Much More Expensive in Some States Than Others?
item.description: Shipping, taxes, competition and environmental regulations all play a part in the cost of gasoline. That can lead to stark differences in pump prices.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: The national average gas price has soared about 50 percent since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall on Hope U.S.-Iran War Turmoil in the Gulf May Be Easing
item.description: Oil prices fell and stocks rose after President Trump’s about-face to pause the U.S. operation to escort commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images

item.title: Trump and Rubio Insist Iran War Is Over, Even as Missiles Fly During Cease-Fire
item.description: The White House is turning to rhetorical leaps as President Trump tries to put the biggest political crisis of his presidency behind him.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Despite Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s declaration that the objectives of the war have been accomplished, they clearly have not.

item.title: Iran’s Oil Sector and Economy Are Under Pressure as U.S. Blockade Bites
item.description: Tehran says it can endure the pain, but its oil may soon have nowhere to go.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu Agency, via Getty Images
item.media:description: With the United States blockading Iranian shipping, Iran risks running out of room to store oil in places like Kharg Island.

item.title: Macron Wants U.S. and Iran to Open Strait While They Negotiate
item.description: Emmanuel Macron, the French president, hopes the Strait of Hormuz can reopen even before the U.S.-Iran war formally ends. His plan has many pitfalls.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Karen Minasyan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Emmanuel Macron of France has shown frustration about the economic costs of the blocked strait.

item.title: Ted Turner, Creator of CNN and the 24-Hour News Cycle, Dies at 87
item.description: As one of the most important figures in media history, he oversaw a vast cable empire of news, sports and entertainment channels.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Kandell
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ted Turner was a visionary media mogul who dominated the cable television industry, and extended his reach into movies, major league baseball and even yachting.

item.title: Rupert Murdoch, David Zaslav and More Media Moguls Remember Ted Turner, CNN’s Founder
item.description: The CNN founder’s death was met with encomiums from leaders in the media industry, which he helped reinvent. “He will be remembered for generations,” said one.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Brian F Alpert/ZUMA Press Wire, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: Ted Turner in the 1980s.

item.title: Tennessee Republicans Unveil New Congressional Map Carving Up Majority-Black House District
item.description: The General Assembly is expected to quickly approve the map, which slices up Memphis, a majority-Black city that makes up most of the state’s lone Democratic district.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tennessee legislators are seen on the House floor in Nashville on Wednesday morning, where they were expected to unveil a new House map that affects the city of Memphis.

item.title: Obama Talks Trump, Mamdani, Aliens and More in Colbert Interview
item.description: The former president was critical of President Trump, without ever naming him. Mr. Obama also urged Democrats to be plain-spoken, and talked about aliens.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

item.title: Tofersen, a New Treatment for A.L.S., Reverses Symptoms for Some
item.description: The drug is for a small subset of patients. But evidence that breathing and strength can get better for some of them is remarkable for a paralyzing, usually fatal disease.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Eva Marie Uzcategui for The New York Times

item.title: A Look Inside the Case That Enshrined Political Power for Billionaires
item.description: After Watergate, Congress tried to curtail the role of money in politics. But a pivotal Supreme Court case nipped it in the bud. Years later, new details are emerging on how wealthy Americans were conferred with a “right to spend” on elections.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Keystone Press Agency/ZUMA Press Wire, via Reuters
item.media:description: A landmark Supreme Court case laid the groundwork for the tremendous amounts spent on today’s elections.

item.title: Weather Service Races to Rehire as Storm Season Arrives
item.description: After deep cuts last year, the agency is hiring hundreds. But fears linger that it isn’t equipped for imminent tornado and hurricane threats.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Judson Jones and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Julio Cortez/Associated Press
item.media:description: A member of the National Weather Service surveying a storm-damaged area in Mineral Wells, Texas, last week.

item.title: Howard Lutnick Faces Questions From Congress About Epstein Ties
item.description: The commerce secretary was in a closed-door session on Wednesday with the House Oversight Committee.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on Capitol Hill in April. He is one of the highest-profile cabinet members to come under scrutiny in connection with Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Her Self-Experiment with Drug Detox Almost Broke Her
item.description: Against expert advice, people are using new and unpredictable synthetic drugs to experiment on themselves in hopes of becoming free of addiction.
item.dc:creator: Matt Richtel and Meridith Kohut
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times

item.title: Leo XIV Is the First Pope From the U.S. He’s Making That an Asset.
item.description: A year into his papacy, Leo has shown a readiness to challenge Washington, defying any concerns about the election of an American pontiff.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Gregorio Borgia/Associated Press
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV arriving at St. Peter’s Square in the Vatican on Wednesday.

item.title: What Happened When Pope Leo XIV Had to Call Customer Service
item.description: Spoiler alert: There was no miracle.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Alessandra Tarantino/Associated Press
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV has a palace, the Swiss Guard and the popemobile. But he also has hassles, just like everyone else.

item.title: The Longevity Secrets Helping Athletes Blow Past the Limits of Age
item.description: With cutting-edge sports medicine and sci-fi gadgetry, more and more athletes are figuring out how to extend their careers.
item.dc:creator: Devin Gordon and Dina Litovsky

item.title: 3 People Are Evacuated From Cruise Ship With Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: Three people with possible symptoms of the disease were evacuated on Wednesday morning from the Dutch-flagged MV Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Aimee Ortiz
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The MV Hondius off the port of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde, on Wednesday.

item.title: Guy Goma’s Accidental BBC Interview Lives On After 20 Years
item.description: Guy Goma thought he was interviewing for an I.T. job when he found himself on air on the BBC. What came next was familiar to anyone who has been unprepared at work.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe

item.title: Artifacts from the Titanic May Soon Be Auctioned
item.description: The last time a sale was proposed, the idea drew objections from the U.S. and French governments, and from UNESCO and other cultural institutions.
item.dc:creator: Marc Davis

item.title: In ‘Rocky Horror,’ Luke Evans Finds His Ballad of Sexual Liberation
item.description: What does it take to play Frank-N-Furter in “The Rocky Horror Show” on Broadway? Fishnets, five-inch heels, and an endless supply of glitter.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Justin J Wee
item.media:credit: Justin J Wee for The New York Times

item.title: Neil deGrasse Tyson: Give Us the Aliens
item.description: Is that too much to ask for?
item.dc:creator: Neil deGrasse Tyson
item.media:credit: Illustration by Sam Whitney/The New York Times

item.title: John Roberts Believes in an America That Doesn’t Exist
item.description: Guess who benefits from a “colorblind Constitution.”
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: The Cruise From Hell
item.description: We look into the hantavirus crisis onboard the Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius near Cape Verde yesterday.

item.title: Tell Us How High Gas Prices Have Affected Your Finances
item.description: With fuel costs soaring from the war with Iran, we want to hear how you’re feeling about the U.S. economy.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Gas prices have soared since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Office of Virginia State Senator Louise Lucas Raided by F.B.I. Agents
item.description: Two people familiar with the case said the search of a Democratic lawmaker’s office was related to a Biden-era investigation of possible corruption and bribery related to marijuana businesses.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Ryan M. Kelly/Associated Press
item.media:description: State Senator Louise Lucas in the legislative chamber in Richmond this year.

item.title: Reclaiming the Name of the Black Hero Who Inspired ‘Uncle Tom’s Cabin’
item.description: After 42 years of slavery, Josiah Henson escaped to Canada, where he wrote a memoir, founded a school and led others to freedom. But his home long bore the name “Uncle Tom,” to the offense of many.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Ian Willms
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Henson House at the Josiah Henson Museum of African-Canadian History in Dresden, Canada.

item.title: An Audit Has Fired Up Massachusetts Voters. Yes, Really.
item.description: Voters approved a ballot measure in 2024 authorizing Diana DiZoglio, the state auditor, to closely scrutinize the State Legislature. Lawmakers are resisting.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Diana DiZoglio, a Democrat, has faced stiff resistance from lawmakers in her own party.

item.title: Guido Reichstadter Comes Down From Frederick Douglass Memorial Bridge After 6 Days
item.description: Guido Reichstadter, a 45-year-old former jeweler, began sitting atop the Frederick Douglass Memorial Bridge on May 1 to protest A.I. development and the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Julia Demaree-Nikhinson/Associated Press
item.media:description: Emergency personnel remove Guido Reichstadter, 45, from the Frederick Douglass Memorial Bridge on Wednesday.
